summary: Gruber examines the implications of South Korea's law requiring Apple and Google to allow third-party payment processing for in-app purchases. He argues that most typical users prefer the controlled app store experience over PC-like openness, and that legislators are primarily swayed by business arguments without understanding technical consequences. While he concedes Apple's parental controls argument is sophistry since APIs could be built to support third-party payments, he raises a more substantive concern: the potential loss of Apple's centralized subscription management system, which makes tracking and canceling subscriptions trivially easy compared to dealing with each provider individually.
key_points: The push to open app store payments effectively means making phones work more like PCs, which most regular users don't actually want
Apple's claim that parental controls would suffer is sophistry — they could build APIs to support third-party payments through existing parental control systems
The most significant user-facing concern is losing centralized subscription management, where Apple makes tracking and canceling subscriptions trivially easy
Only two groups want phones to work more like PCs: technically-savvy users confident in their own judgment, and business-minded people focused on revenue splits
The quality of user experience on every computing platform is directly correlated to the amount of control exerted by its platform owner
money_quotes: The masses are not clamoring for the app stores to be opened up. These arguments over app stores are entirely inside baseball for the technical and business classes.
Anyone who claims to know how such a scenario will turn out is full of shit.

The quality of the user experience of every computing platform is directly correlated to the amount of control exerted by its platform owner. The current state of the ownerless world wide web speaks for itself.

Boy, I sure wish iPhone apps and games could ask me for my credit card number to make purchases, and that the overall experience of using apps was more like the anything-goes nature of using the web or my desktop computer.

To cancel a subscription to The New York Times, you need to call them on the phone or engage in an online chat with a 'customer service representative' whose full-time job is convincing people not to cancel their subscription.
Adding support for third-party payment processing for in-app purchases in no way prevents Apple and Google from providing robust parental controls to approve kids' in-app purchases.
Apple's subscription system is so useful, so trustworthy, and so beneficial to my peace of mind that as a general rule I only subscribe to anything through it.
key_insight: The most underappreciated casualty of mandating third-party payment processing would be Apple's centralized subscription management — a genuinely user-beneficial system that makes subscriptions easy to track and cancel, unlike the dark-pattern-laden alternatives providers would prefer.
